children.name: Agelaius
children.description: The red-winged and yellow-winged blackbirds — medium-sized icterids named for their distinctive shoulder patches. Males are black with bold red or yellow epaulets, which they flash aggressively during territorial displays. They are highly polygynous, with dominant males defending territories that attract multiple females, who build nests woven into cattails and marsh vegetation.

children.name: Agelaius phoeniceus
children.description: One of the most abundant and familiar birds in North America, found in virtually every marsh, wet meadow, and roadside ditch across the continent. The male is glossy black with brilliant red shoulder patches bordered by yellow; the female is heavily streaked brown, resembling a large sparrow. Its conk-la-ree! song and piercing alarm calls are defining sounds of North American wetlands.

children.name: Quiscalus
children.description: The grackles — large, glossy blackbirds with long, keeled tails and pale yellow eyes. They are among the most intelligent of all songbirds, capable of solving complex puzzles and remembering individual human faces. Grackles are highly social, forming enormous noisy roosts, and have adapted brilliantly to urban and suburban environments.

children.name: Quiscalus quiscula
children.description: A large, iridescent blackbird with a long, keel-shaped tail and pale yellow eyes. Its glossy plumage shimmers with purple, bronze, and green in direct sunlight. It is a familiar bird across eastern North America, often seen in parking lots, agricultural fields, and suburban lawns where it forages with a distinctive stiff-legged walk. Its song is a harsh, mechanical 'readle-reek' accompanied by wing-fluttering.

children.name: Quiscalus mexicanus
children.description: A spectacularly long-tailed blackbird of the Americas, the male reaching up to 43 cm in length thanks to its oversized tail. It has undergone a dramatic range expansion northward in the last century, colonising much of the southern and central United States. Males produce an extraordinary array of whistles, clicks, and mechanical sounds, often singing from the highest available perch.

children.name: Quiscalus major
children.description: A large grackle restricted to salt and brackish marshes along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the United States. The male has a distinctive deep purplish-blue gloss and an enormously long, boat-shaped tail that gives the species its name. It is maritime in habit, nesting in marsh vegetation and foraging along shorelines, and is never found far from the coast.

children.name: Molothrus
children.description: The cowbirds — obligate brood parasites that lay their eggs in the nests of other passerine species. They are named for their historical association with bison herds, following the great herds to feed on insects flushed by their movement. A single female may lay several dozen eggs in a season, each in a different host nest, never building a nest or rearing her own young.

children.name: Molothrus ater
children.description: North America's most widespread brood parasite, known to have exploited over 200 host species. The male has a glossy black body with a brown head; the female is plain grey-brown. Historically it followed bison herds across the Great Plains, but deforestation and agriculture have vastly expanded its range, to the detriment of many forest-nesting warblers and vireos that are common hosts.

children.name: Icterus
children.description: The New World orioles — brilliantly coloured blackbirds with a striking contrast of black and bright yellow, orange, or chestnut. They build remarkable hanging pouch nests woven from plant fibres, suspended from branch tips. Males sing rich, fluty whistles that carry through the treetops. They are primarily frugivorous and insectivorous, and many species undertake long migrations between breeding and wintering grounds.

children.name: Icterus galbula
children.description: The most familiar oriole of eastern North America, named for the black-and-orange colours of the Baltimore family coat of arms. The male flames with brilliant orange underparts and a rich black hood and back. It weaves an intricate hanging nest of plant fibres, hair, and string, suspended from elm, maple, and poplar branches. Its rich, whistled song is a beloved sound of eastern spring.

children.name: Icterus spurius
children.description: The smallest North American oriole, the male is a rich chestnut-and-black unlike the orange of the Baltimore Oriole. It prefers open woodland, orchards, and farmland edges, building a hanging basket nest more loosely woven than that of its larger relative. It is one of the earliest Neotropical migrants to return to its North American breeding grounds in spring.

children.name: Xanthocephalus
children.description: The yellow-headed blackbird — a striking monotypic genus named for the male's vivid yellow head and breast. It breeds in the marshes of western and central North America, often alongside Red-winged Blackbirds. Males defend territories in deep-water cattail marshes and perform impressive flight displays, singing a grating, strained song while hanging from cattail stems.

children.name: Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus
children.description: One of the most striking American songbirds: the male has a brilliant yellow hood and breast contrasting with a jet-black body and white wing patches. It breeds in large marshes across western and central North America, where its harsh, croaking song and metallic calls carry across the water. Females are brown with a dull yellow throat, much less conspicuous than the males.

children.name: Dolichonyx
children.description: The bobolink — a monotypic genus of Grassland icterid with one of the longest migrations of any passerine. The male's breeding plumage is unique among North American birds: black below and on the head, with a buff nape and white back and rump. The species is named for its bubbling, ecstatic song, which pours forth in sustained, tumbling phrases from the male's flight display over hayfields and prairies.

children.name: Dolichonyx oryzivorus
children.description: A remarkable grassland bird that undertakes one of the longest migrations of any New World passerine — up to 20,000 km round trip from the prairies of North America to the pampas of Argentina. The male in breeding plumage is striking: black underparts and head, buff nape, white back and rump. Its bubbling, tumbling song is a hallmark of native prairies and hayfields, but early mowing threatens its breeding success.

children.name: Sturnella
children.description: The meadowlarks — stocky, ground-dwelling icterids with bright yellow underparts and a bold black V on the breast. Despite their name they are not larks but true blackbirds, and are among the most iconic birds of American grasslands. Their clear, fluting songs carry across open fields, and they are more often heard than seen, perching on fence posts and low trees to deliver their melodies.

children.name: Sturnella magna
children.description: The eastern counterpart of the grassland meadowlark pair, with a clear, plaintive song of two to five whistled notes, the last descending. It inhabits pastures, meadows, and prairies across eastern North America, Central America, and into northern South America. Like its western relative, it walks on the ground probing soil for insects and sings from prominent perches. It has declined sharply due to intensive agriculture.

children.name: Sturnella neglecta
children.description: Nearly identical in appearance to the Eastern Meadowlark but told apart by its song: a rich, fluting, seven-note warble that descends the scale in a sweet, bubbling phrase. It ranges across western and central North America from Canada to Mexico. The state bird of six US states, it is a symbol of the American prairie. Its scientific name neglecta refers to its long being overlooked as distinct from the Eastern species.

children.name: Euphagus
children.description: The American blackbirds — a small genus of two species that bridge the gap between grackles and true blackbirds. They are medium-sized icterids with glossy black plumage, pale eyes, and relatively slender bills. One species is a common bird of towns and farms, while the other has suffered a dramatic and poorly understood population decline.

children.name: Euphagus cyanocephalus
children.description: A common and adaptable blackbird of western and central North America, often found in parking lots, parks, and agricultural areas where it forages on the ground with a jaunty walk. The male is glossy black with a purple-green iridescence and a pale yellow eye; the female is plain grey-brown. It is highly gregarious, forming large mixed flocks with other icterids outside the breeding season.

children.name: Euphagus carolinus
children.description: A boreal forest blackbird that has experienced one of the most severe population declines of any North American bird — a 90% reduction since the 1960s for reasons that remain unclear. In winter, its glossy black feathers acquire rusty-brown tips, giving the species its common name. It breeds in beaver ponds, swamps, and wet boreal forests across Canada and Alaska.
